baseline_dominance.justification: The construction removes the AS from the authorization hot path entirely — post-enrollment, the agent self-proves to each RS via PLONK against on-chain roots with no token issuance step. §8 explicitly names this structural impossibility: OAuth/OIDC places the AS on the critical path of every authorization, and no composition of RFCs (7662, 8693, 8707, 9449) can replicate AS-invisibility because every mitigation (PPID, DPoP, BBS+) operates below the AS. The rfc7662_advocate's Attack 4 concedes the categorical difference is real, only objecting that §8 overstates baseline weakness on traffic-frequency vs topology. The capability — AS never learns which RSes the agent contacts — is provably absent from the baseline.

formal_security_argument.justification: The construction provides a threat model (§3.1: adversarial AS, colluding RS coalition, network observer), names four cryptographic assumptions (Poseidon PRF, Poseidon CR, PLONK knowledge soundness, Baby Jubjub DL), defines the IND-UNL-AS game (§3.2), and sketches a reduction (§4.2). However, the cryptographer's Attack 1 shows the reduction has a simulator gap: B cannot produce challenge proofs without the PLONK SRS trapdoor, making the concrete bound unproven. Attack 3 shows the IND-UNL-AS game captures single-proof scope indistinguishability, not the cross-session linkability that is the actual claimed property against colluding RSes — the game is inconsistent with the §3.1 threat model. The collusion resistance argument in §4.3 is prose, not a formal game with reduction. Threat model is stated and assumptions are named, but the reduction is incomplete and the game is misaligned with the stated threat.

implementability.justification: Every construction element maps to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon2/Poseidon5 hashes, EdDSAPoseidonVerifier on Baby Jubjub, BinaryMerkleRoot(20), PLONK with pot16.ptau universal setup. §6 provides a detailed constraint breakdown totaling ~17,724 constraints, well within the 2^16 (65,536) pot16.ptau budget. Proving time targets are stated: <3s PLONK on commodity hardware, <2s Groth16 with rapidsnark. The ScopedDelegation extension adds ~1,200 constraints (total ~19,200), still within budget. No new primitives are introduced — scopeBlindingSecret is a standard field element managed identically to the human secret scalar, and the nullifier derivation reuses the existing Poseidon2(scope, secret) pattern from HumanUniqueness.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 found multiple attacks the construction cannot answer. The most devastating: (1) scopeCommitment is a public output identical across all scopes for the same agent — colluding RSes trivially link proofs by comparing this value, directly contradicting §4.3's claim that RSes don't see it. Found independently by cryptographer (Attack 2), cu_ciso (Attack 3), spiffe_engineer (Attack 3), and rfc7662_advocate (Attack 1). The construction's own §2.2 and §4.3 are internally inconsistent. (2) The suggested scopeBlindingSecret derivation Poseidon2(operatorPrivKey, 'bolyra-scope-blind') means the AS-as-operator (NFCU in §7) can precompute all nullifiers for every agent it enrolled, destroying the headline claim. Found by cryptographer (Attack 4), rfc7662_advocate (Attack 3), auth0_pm (Attack 3). (3) The IND-UNL-AS reduction has a simulator gap (cryptographer Attack 1). These are not mitigated — they break the core unlinkability claim as specified.

scenario_fit.justification: Two concrete scenarios are named with specific stakeholders: cross-CU member agent (NFCU, Amazon, Costco) and healthcare delegation (Kaiser, Quest, Cedars-Sinai). The CU scenario identifies a real regulatory tension — NFCU-as-AS learning the merchant graph. However, auth0_pm (Attack 1) and cu_ciso (Attack 1) demonstrate that the construction's core property (destroying AS visibility) directly conflicts with NCUA Part 748 Appendix A §III.C.4, GLBA Safeguards Rule §314.4(h), and FFIEC CAT Domain 3, which require the CU to maintain audit trails of agent authorizations. The construction offers no selective-disclosure audit mode or regulatory trapdoor. The lead deployment scenario's regulatory requirements contradict the construction's value proposition, making the scenario self-defeating as specified.

gaps: Remove scopeCommitment from ScopedAgentAuth public outputs — it is a stable cross-scope agent fingerprint that trivially breaks collusion resistance. Move it to a separate on-chain-only delegation verification circuit.

Rearchitect scopeBlindingSecret derivation: must use agent-local entropy unknown to the operator/AS, not Poseidon2(operatorPrivKey, salt). The current derivation makes every AS-as-issuer deployment trivially omniscient.

Fix the reduction simulator gap: either work in the simulation-extractable PLONK model with explicit trapdoor access, or restructure the reduction so B uses honest witnesses throughout and embeds the PRF challenge only in nullifier comparison.

Define a cross-session linkability game (not just single-proof scope indistinguishability) that captures the colluding-RS threat stated in §3.1. The IND-UNL-AS game is misaligned with the actual threat model.

Add a regulatory selective-disclosure mechanism (e.g., regulator-keyed audit circuit or compliance HSM escrow) so the CU deployment scenario does not self-defeat against NCUA/GLBA/FFIEC audit trail requirements.
Bound minimum anonymity set size as a deployment parameter — agentMerkleRoot narrows the population to O(30) candidates in sparse trees, creating a practical linkability vector unaddressed in §3.
Define a scopeBlindingSecret rotation protocol with bounded exposure window and forward secrecy properties.
Specify a scopeId namespace authority to prevent collision-based forced linkage between independent RSes.
